2004-05-01.title: World Briefing | The Americas: United Nations: Peacekeepers For Haiti
2004-05-01.abstract: United Nations Security Council unanimously approves peacekeeping force for Haiti consisting of 6,700 soldiers and 1,622 police officers; UN force will relieve US troops on June 1 (S)

2004-05-01.title: Marines Transfer Falluja Positions to an Iraqi Force
2004-05-01.abstract: Even as efforts to restore calm were under way, skirmishes were reported and a suicide bomber killed two marines.

2004-05-01.title: MARINES TRANSFER FALLUJA POSITIONS TO AN IRAQI FORCE
2004-05-01.abstract: Marines withdraw from most positions they took during three-week siege of Falluja, as US military begins handing over control of security there to Jasim Muhammad Saleh, former Iraqi general under Saddam Hussein and up to 1,100 men under his command; suicide bomber kills two marines and wounds six others; over 1,000 Iraqis in Najaf protest against Bush administration's decision to loosen ban on participation in government by members of once-ruling Baath Party; map; photos (M)

2004-05-01.title: Bush Voices 'Disgust' at Abuse of Iraqi Prisoners
2004-05-01.abstract: Pres Bush says he is deeply disgusted by reports that Iraqi detainees were abused by US military police at Abu Ghraib prison, and vows that soldiers found to be at fault will be punished; speaks on day that photographs circulate around world showing US soldiers laughing as naked Iraqi prisoners are forced into humiliating positions; photographs draw particular anger in Arab world just as US military in Iraq is seeking to pacify rising insurgency and gain trust of larger percentage of local population ahead of passing sovereignty to new government in Baghdad on June 30; military is considering action against Brig Gen Janis Karpinski, senior officer at prison when abuse occurred against 20 detainees in Nov and Dec; photos (M)

2004-05-01.title: Macedonia Charges Ex-Official In Staging of Antiterror Killings
2004-05-01.abstract: Macedonia charges former Interior Min Ljube Boskovski with staging killing of seven South Asian migrants in March 2002 in attempt to show US that government was actively supporting campaign against terror; Western diplomats describe killings as crude attempt by government to win free hand to deal harshly with Macedonia's ethnic Albanian minority (M)

2004-05-02.title: U.S. Hostage Is Free After Escaping; 9 G.I.'s Die in Iraq
2004-05-02.abstract: A U.S. contractor taken hostage last month escaped from his captors on a day when nine more U.S. soldiers died in Iraq.

2004-05-02.title: Photographs Show Abuse Of Prisoners By British
2004-05-02.abstract: British Defense Ministry begins investigation into allegations of abuse of prisoners in Basra after soldiers from Queen's Lancashire Regiment bring photographs of abuse to Daily Mirror newspaper; Prime Min Tony Blair calls images 'completely and utterly unacceptable' (S)

2004-05-02.title: Driving in Kenya Can Be Hazardous to Your Health
2004-05-02.abstract: Kenya estimates 2,000 people are killed in road accidents each year; fatality rate is about 55 deaths per 10,000 motor vehicles, while US rate is two deaths per 10,000 vehicles; Kenya spends $76 million each year to care for accident victims; leading advocate of new safer driving program is Mary Mwangangi, traffic commandant of Kenya's Police Department; Mwangangi broke both arms and both legs in collision with speeding truck in 2003; photos (M)

2004-05-02.title: OFFICER SUGGESTS IRAQI JAIL ABUSE WAS ENCOURAGED
2004-05-02.abstract: Army Reserve Gen Janis Karpinski, whose soldiers were photographed as they abused Iraqi prisoners at Abu Ghraib, says she knew nothing about abuse until after it occurred and that she is 'sickened' by photos; says cellblock where abuse occurred was under tight control of Army military intelligence officers who may have encouraged abuse, and suggestion appears largely supported in still-classified Army report; New Yorker magazine article by Seymour M Hersh says report by Maj Gen Antonio M Taguba found that reservist military police at prison were urged to prepare prisoners for interrogation; indicates far more wide-ranging and systematic pattern of abuse than previously reported and reveals litany of cruelties on part of soldiers; photo (M)

2004-05-02.title: As a New Iraqi Force Goes to Work in Falluja, Questions Arise About Its Leader's Record
2004-05-02.abstract: Maj Gen Jasim Muhammad Saleh commands new Iraqi force approved to pacify Falluja; US military has offered conflicting statements about Saleh's past in Saddam Hussein's elite Republican Guard; Marines cite creation of new Iraqi force as beginning of military partnership to bring stability and peace to Iraq, while some Iraqis in Falluja celebrate replacement of American with Iraqi troops as victory; photo (M)

2004-05-03.title: Hostage Is Free; 9 G.I.'s Killed in Iraq Attacks
2004-05-03.abstract: A U.S. contractor taken hostage last month escaped, and nine soldiers died in violence across central and northern Iraq.

2004-05-03.title: Iraqi Force In Falluja, With City Still on Brink
2004-05-03.abstract: Falluja is balancing on brink between conflict and recovery, after weeks of battling between American marines and insurgents; which way things go may be determined in next few days, as new Iraqi security force struggles to bring order to city after fighting that killed more than 600 residents; photos (M)

2004-05-03.title: Afghan Faction Reaches Out to Karzai
2004-05-03.abstract: Senior members of Islamic Party, mujahedeen party allied with Afghanistan's former Taliban rulers and opposed to presence of American forces, denounce violence and say they want to join political process; announcement, made after months of negotiations with Pres Hamid Karzai, is seen as major victory for him; party's leader is Gulbuddin Hekmatyar, one of most wanted men by US-led forces for terrorist attacks against troops; delegation meeting with Karzai denounces terrorism and violence and says it has no contact with Hekmatyar (M)

2004-05-03.title: With Killer's Execution, Karzai Signals Move on Warlords
2004-05-03.abstract: Abdullah Shah, notorious mujahedeen commander, is first man to be executed in two years since Pres Hamid Karzai established new Afghan government; Afghans welcome execution as sign that government is finally moving against criminal warlords, but it raises concerns among human rights groups and reveals shortcomings in judicial system under Karzai's Transitional Administration; rights groups say Shah was not given fair trial and was falsely accused of being notorious killer; there is some speculation that more powerful warlords orchestrated case against Shah; photos (M)

2004-05-03.title: Mexico and Peru Withdraw Ambassadors From Cuba
2004-05-03.abstract: Mexico withdrews its ambassador from Cuba after weeklong exchange of angry diplomatic dispatches; relationship between former allies is at its lowest point in recent years; Mexican officials accuse Cuban government of words and actions that infringe on Mexican sovereignty, indicating spying; Pres Fidel Castro's May Day speech attacked Mexico for voting in favor of United Nations resolution that criticized Cuba's record on human rights; Peru also withdraws its ambassador from Cuba, citing similar remarks Castro made in same speech about Peru's vote (M)

2004-05-04.title: Stolen Fertilizer Is Sought by French Police
2004-05-04.abstract: French police are scrambling to locate 1,100 pounds of fertilizer that could be used to make a powerful bomb after the material was discovered missing on Monday.

2004-05-04.title: Contract Workers Implicated in February Army Report on Prison Abuse Remain on the Job
2004-05-04.abstract: Companies that employ contract workers in Iraq say they have heard nothing fron Pentagon two months after classified Army report that implicated two contract workers in abuse of Iraqi prisoners; say they have not removed any employees from Iraq; Army report recommended 'termination of employment' for one employee and official reprimand for other; military spokesmen in Washington and Baghdad say they do not know why report had not been conveyed to companies, CACI and Titan; CACI issues statement defending its employees and saying they are well-trained former military personnel; Titan, which supplies translators, says it has no contracts that involve physical handling of prisoners (M)

2004-05-04.title: Out of sight, American expatriates struggle for political clout : Trying to make themselves counted
2004-05-04.abstract: WASHINGTON:—  As Lucy Laederich recalls, it was an offhand question during a U.S. Census Bureau meeting to examine the feasibility of counting Americans abroad that got her thinking about the conundrum facing overseas citizens.

2004-05-04.title: In Sudan, Militiamen on Horses Uproot a Million
2004-05-04.abstract: Loose bands of Arab fighters called Janjawed are purging villages of western Sudan of their darker-skinned black African inhabitants; United Nations estimates million people have been displaced in conflict pitting Arab nomads and herders against settled black African farmers; for 20 years rebels in southern Sudan have sought to topple Arab-dominated government in north; two million people died in that larger conflict, and peace agreement is considered near; now separate rebellion has been initiated by two rebel groups; Sudan's decades-old civil war, which was much about religion, is now over ethnicity and resources, pitting Muslim against Muslim; Janjaweed ride camels and horses and use automatic weapons against those they come across; they ride into villages en masse and shoot anyone in sight; as they torch and loot, villagers flee; UN says crisis in western Sudan will last another 18 months--if government manages to disarm men on horseback soon; it remains to be seen whether lawlessness will be tamed; photos; map (L)

2004-05-04.title: Army Conducting 20 Criminal Inquiries in Treatment of Iraqis
2004-05-04.abstract: The criminal inquiries came as the furor over mistreatment of some Iraqi captives continued to grow.

2004-05-05.title: Ambushes by Taliban Gunmen Kill 10 Policemen and Soldiers
2004-05-05.abstract: Taliban gunmen kill at least 10 Afghan policemen and soldiers in two ambushes in southern Afghanistan; attacks, in Zabul Province and Mianishin, are part of surge of violence by Taliban and other insurgents (S)

2004-05-05.title: Display of Warships Signals Hong Kong of China's Resolve
2004-05-05.abstract: Chinese warships sailed down Victoria Harbor today as democracy advocates in Hong Kong say they face growing intimidation.

2004-05-05.title: Saudis Identify Militant as Leader of Fatal Attack
2004-05-05.abstract: Saudi authorities say Mustafa Abdulqader Abed al-Ansari, who is linked to London-based Saudi opposition group Movement for Islamic Reform in Arabia, led terrorist attack on offices of ABB Lummus, Yanbu, that killed six people; say he, his brother and two nephews were killed in gun battle with security forces; Min Saud al-Faisal charges group is financed by Israeli interests, charge denied by group's leader Saad al-Faqih, who says it seeks to oust Saud royal family but does not endorse violence (M)

2004-05-05.title: World Briefing | Africa: Zimbabwe: Private Schools Shut Over High Fees
2004-05-05.abstract: Zimbabwe's Education Minister Aeneas Chigwedere closes 46 private schools claiming raises in tuition are aimed at keeping black students away (S)

2004-05-06.title: Low-Tech or High, Jobs Are Scarce in India's Boom
2004-05-06.abstract: India's economy is spawning growing middle class, but its reforms and conditions are also reducing prospects of some of its citizens; country is struggling to generate jobs; employment problem could bring downfall of ruling party, which is seeking re-election on strength of economic growth; public sector, once a stalwart of security, has lost 4.5 million jobs in six years; government recruitment is frozen and jobs once handled by civil servants are being handed over to private contractors, who offer lower wages and few benefits; for many Indians, dismantling of quasi-socialist economy and growing globalization have meant only trickle-down of raised expectations and lowered opportunity; economists say official unemployment figure of 8 percent masks far higher real rate; photos; map (M)

2004-05-06.title: BUSH, ON ARAB TV, DENOUNCES ABUSE OF IRAQI CAPTIVES
2004-05-06.abstract: Pres Bush goes on Arab television in effort to limit diplomatic damage from abuse of Iraqi prisoners by American soldiers, saying mistreatment 'does not represent the America that I know'; offers no apology, but pledges to bring all facts into open and says guilty would be held accountable; abuse case has left Bush administration on defensive; State Dept officials acknowledge that they put off release of annual report on human rights abuses around world because they did not want to face awkward questions about how United States could point fingers at time when its own record has been sullied; in Congress, senior members of both parties express anger at failure of Pentagon to keep Congress fully informed about case; Sen John W Warner, Republican chairman of Armed Services Committee, summons Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld to public hearing on May 7; national security advisor Condoleezza Rice and deputy secretary of state Richard L Armitage apologize in their interviews with Arab broadcasters; new commandant of Abu Ghraib prison, Maj Gen Geoffrey Miller, also apologizes to Iraqi people; Bush's interviews are broadcast on Al Hurra, US government-sponsored network, and Al Arabiya, Dubai-based broadcaster; photo (M)

2004-05-06.title: U.S. Troops Start Major Attacks On Shiite Insurgents in 2 Cities
2004-05-06.abstract: American military begins its first major assault against Shiite insurgents, striking at enclaves in Karbala and Diwaniya in effort to regain control in southern Iraq; coordinated attacks begin hours after powerful Shiite politicians and religious leaders urge rebellious cleric Moktada al-Sadr to withdraw his militia from Karbala and Najaf; soldiers kill at least 10 Iraqi fighters and capture 20 people in first attack in Karbala; American soldier is killed when two insurgents run checkpoint and ram truck into his Humvee; Sadr spokesman says nine militiamen are killed in Diwaniya; American assault on Karbala takes place southwest of two Shiite shrines, and commanders say they are trying to make precise attacks; town leaders do not protest, and dozens of families stand outside their homes watching convoy as it rolls toward battle; photo (M)

2004-05-06.title: World Briefing | Asia: India: Election Violence
2004-05-06.abstract: Three people die in late stage of three-week-long national elections in India; early predictions suggest that governing Hindu nationalist party will win same number of Parliament seats as it now holds (S)

2004-05-07.title: Bush Proposes a Plan to Aid Opponents of Castro in Cuba
2004-05-07.abstract: Pres Bush announces plan to use military aircraft to help American broadcasters reach Cuba and to increase sharply money for Cuban critics of Pres Fidel Castro's government; measures, which include further restrictions on travel and cash transfers to Cuba, are embraced by Republican Cuban-American lawmakers and other adherents of hard-line position toward Havana; Rep Robert Menendez, lone Cuban-American Democrat in Congress, accuses Bush of pandering to Cuban exiles in South Florida, who are considered central to his re-election strategy; other critics question plan's expense; Sen Max Baucus claims new plan amounts to misuse of taxpayer money (M)

2004-05-07.title: U.S Troops in Heavy Fighting Around 2 Shiite Holy Cities
2004-05-07.abstract: American soldiers killed at least two dozen insurgents in Karbala and Najaf over a 24-hour period, officials said today.

2004-05-07.title: World Briefing | Europe: Turkey: Army Lashes Out At Education Reform Plan
2004-05-07.abstract: Turkish miltary criticizes government's planned educational reforms that political analysts suggest would promote more involvement of Islamists in secular careers (S)

2004-05-07.title: Blast Rips Through Shiite Mosque in Pakistan, Killing at Least 9
2004-05-07.abstract: After the blast, angry groups of young men roamed the city, setting fire to a gas station and destroying cars.

2004-05-08.title: As oil tops $40, response is muted
2004-05-08.abstract: PARIS:—  The price of oil rose above $40 a barrel on Friday for the first time since the build-up to the Gulf war of 1991, stoking concerns that global economic growth might be curbed by higher energy costs.

2004-05-08.title: Uprooted Sudanese Balk at Invitation to Return Home
2004-05-08.abstract: Geneina, Sudan, Gov Sulieman Abdallah Adam says Darfur is safe again and orders million refugees amassed in makeshift camps across western Sudan to head back to their villages; hundreds of black Africans chased off their land by Arab militias and government troops say they do not believe Adam and will flout his order; map; photo (M)

2004-05-08.title: Mistreatment of Prisoners Is Called Routine in U.S.
2004-05-08.abstract: Corrections officials, inmates and human rights advocates say physical and sexual abuse of prisoners, similar to what has been uncovered in Iraq, takes place in American prisons with little public knowledge or concern; corrections experts note that Lane McCotter, who directed reopening of Abu Ghraib prison and trained guards there, resigned under pressure as director of Utah Corrections Dept in 1997 after inmate died while shackled to chair for 16 hours; McCotter photo (M)

2004-05-08.title: Africans May Claim Much of $3.5 Billion in U.S. Reform Rewards
2004-05-08.abstract: African nations are poised to claim major part of $3.5 billion in US aid under Bush administration plan to reward countries that govern responsibly and invest in their people; administration's Millennium Challenge Corp has selected 16 nations that can receive aid, eight from Africa, three from Latin America and five from Asia and Eurasia (M)

2004-05-08.title: G.I.'S KILL SCORES OF MILITIA FORCES IN 3 IRAQI CITIES
2004-05-08.abstract: US soldiers battle Iraqi insurgents led by rebel Shiite cleric Moktada al-Sadr; reportedly kill at least 25 insurgents in Karbala, 12 in Najaf and at least 41 in two separate battles around Kufa; firefights are most intense since US military started operation in Karbala on May 6 to crush Sadr's thousands-strong militia; Sadr delivers defiant sermon in Kufa that dismisses Pres Bush's expressions of regret for abuse of Iraqi prisoners; map; photos (M)

2004-05-09.title: Hard-Liners In Iran Back Torture Ban
2004-05-09.abstract: Iran's Guardian Council approves law passed by outgoing reformist Parliament that bans torture and extraction of confessions by duress; approval is surprise because Council rejected three earlier bills submitted by Parliament as contrary to Islamic law; Iranian Constitution bans all forms of torture, but practice has been common in prisons; shift by Council may signal desire to work more closely with new conservative Parliament chosen in recent boycotted elections (M)

2004-05-09.title: In Abuse, a Portrayal of Ill-Prepared, Overwhelmed G.I.'s
2004-05-09.abstract: Interviews with soldiers, their relatives, and military commanders and Army reports reveal soldiers called up from National Guard and Army Reserve for military police battalion involved in prisoner abuse at Abu Ghraib, Iraq, prison had little or no training in guarding enemy prisoners and running prison; soldiers found selves in charge of prison at time when increasing rage of anti-American insurgency and desperation of American commanders to glean intelligence magnified pressures on them; chronology of events; photos (L)

2004-05-09.title: China Announces Plan to Curb Rapid Spread of AIDS
2004-05-09.abstract: The government plans to upgrade prevention and education efforts that include holding local officials directly responsible for curbing the fatal disease.

2004-05-09.title: Big Issues Hardly Surfacein Philippines Campaign
2004-05-09.abstract: Filipinos will elect on Monday their new leaders, who will have to wrestle with the problems now plaguing the Philippines.

2004-05-09.title: Militiamen Go on the Offensive in Two Southern Cities
2004-05-09.abstract: Militiamen loyal to rebel cleric Moktada al-Sadr take offensive against British troops in Basra and Amara after al-Sadr aide in Basra gives sermon invoking jihad, offering bounty for dead or captured British soldiers and saying women prisoners may be kept as slaves; men dispersed among demonstrators in Basra open fire, slightly wounding three British soldiers; three more British are wounded after convoy in Amara is ambushed; photo; map (M)

2004-05-10.title: CHECHNYA BOMB KILLS PRESIDENT, A BLOW TO PUTIN
2004-05-10.abstract: Bomb explodes in stadium in Grozny, capital of Chechnya, killing republic's president Akhmad Kadyrov and delivering severe blow to Pres Vladimir V Putin's efforts to end long, deadly conflict in region; Kadyrov, former rebel leader who was elected president in vote widely considered to be fraudulent, was political figure Putin entrusted to wind down war in Chechnya; his death plunges Kemlin's strategy into ominous uncertainty; there is no immediate claim of responsibility for bomb, which kills 13 others and wounds more than 50; explosion occurs as Kadyrov and other Russian and Chechen leaders attend parade and concert in Grozny; Putin announces that Sergei B Abramov, Kremlin-appointed prime minister of Chechnya, will take over as acting president, as stipulated in republic's new constitution, until elections are held sometime before Sept; photos; map (M)

2004-05-10.title: Herbal Drug Widely Embraced In Treating Resistant Malaria
2004-05-10.abstract: World health agencies race to acquire 100 million doses of artemisinin, Chinese herbal drug that has proved strikingly effective against malaria; artemisinin, a compound based on sweet wormwood, cut death rate by 97 percent in malaria epidemic in Vietnam decade ago; is rapidly replacing quinine derivatives and later drugs against which disease has evolved into resistant strains; to protect artemisinin from same fate, it will be given as part of multidrug cocktails; until recently, major donars like United States and Britain had opposed artemisinin's use on wide scale, saying it is too expensive and has not been tested enough on children; Unicef opposed its use during Ethiopian epidemic last year; now almost all donors, Unicef and World Bank have embraced it; World Health Orgn estimates that 100 million doses will be needed by late 2005; malaria, one of leading killers of poor, causes 300 million illnesses a year, and at least 1 million deaths, 90 percent of them in Africa and most of them children under 5; map; chart (M)

2004-05-10.title: Passage Out of Poverty Is Cut Short by Antiterror Snare
2004-05-10.abstract: Macedonian government discloses that police inquiry has found that six Pakistani men and Indian shot dead by local police year ago were not Al Qaeda-linked terrorists but rather illegal immigrants seeking better life in Europe; says their deaths was effort to advance Macedonia's status in campaign against terrorism; spokeswoman for Interior Ministry says incident was staged killing of seven economic migrants by former interior minister; interviews with families of slain men reveal one common wish: to earn better living by going abroad; photo; map (M)

2004-05-10.title: China Unveils Plan to Curb Rapid AIDS Spread
2004-05-10.abstract: Chinese government warns that AIDS is continuing to spread rapidly in country, and announces 'urgent measures' to improve prevention and education efforts that include holding local officials directly responsible for curbing the disease; Deputy Prime Min Wu Yi, who oversees Health Ministry, cautions that China is at crucial stage because disease might soon jump from high-risk groups like prostitutes and intravenous drug users into general population; experts predict that China could have 10 million AIDS patients by 2010 if government fails to adequately fight the disease (M)

2004-05-10.title: U.S. Asks Politicians and Sheiks to Help Rebuild Iraqi Corps in South
2004-05-10.abstract: The move is an effort to rebuild the national security forces that have been decimated by the uprising led by Moktada al-Sadr.

2004-05-11.title: Bush Imposes Export Ban Against Syria
2004-05-11.abstract: President Bush imposed today a series of economic sanctions against Syria, charging that it has failed to take action against terrorist groups fighting Israel.

2004-05-11.title: U.S. Destroys Headquarters Of Rebel Cleric in Baghdad
2004-05-11.abstract: American military says it killed as many as 18 supporters of rebel cleric Moktada al-Sadr as it demolished his Baghdad headquarters during heavy nighttime firefight; says it killed another 18 of his militiamen day earlier; mounting death toll of Sadr's followers includes 41 dead in battle last week in Najaf; clashes with Sadr's followers are erupting daily in cities of Najaf, Kufa, Karbala and Basra amid heightened threats to kill and kidnap foreigners in Iraq; unidentified body appearing to be that of Westerner is found in Baghdad; photo (M)

2004-05-11.title: Palestinians Plan First Municipal Elections for Summer
2004-05-11.abstract: Palestinian Authority says it will begin holding its first municipal elections late this summer, in bid to stem public anger over corruption and mismanagement; under plan, elected representatives will replace mayors and municipal council members who were appointed by Palestinian Authority and who are, in many towns and cites, regarded as incompetent or crooked; elections are also intended to demonstrate to outside world that Palestinian leadership is bent on improving its governance; elections are likely to be held first in relatively tranquil West Bank city of Jericho in Aug, followed by elections in Gaza Strip; in 1996, Palestinian Authority postponed municipal elections, fearing militant group Hamas would do well in them; Hamas has grown stronger during current conflict with Israel, and its leaders say they may compete in municipal elections; photo (M)

2004-05-11.title: British Official Says Soldiers May Soon Face Abuse Charges
2004-05-11.abstract: British Defense Sec Geoff Hoon tells Parliament that British military acted swiftly to investigate allegations of mistreatment by its forces in Iraq and that it is close to bringing charges of brutality against British soldiers in two cases; reveals that in response to complaints raised last summer by Red Cross, British Army stopped practice of putting hoods on Iraqi prisoners in Sept; Hoon speaks day after Prime Min Tony Blair issues first British apology to any Iraqis who suffered mistreatment or degradation during yearlong military campaign in Iraq; Hoon officially challenges authenticity of series of sensational photographs that appear to show hooded Iraqi prisoner being beaten with rifle butts and urinated on; notes Royal Military Police doubts that vehicle in photographs was in Iraq during relevant period (M)

2004-05-11.title: Politicus : In Monaco, they're asking:How about a little respect?
2004-05-11.abstract: MONTE CARLO—  Deep in its spangled and flinty heart, Monaco above all has always wanted to be legit.

2004-05-12.title: A Trusted Troubleshooter, and Overseer of Intelligence Efforts at the Defense Department
2004-05-12.abstract: Profile of Under Sec Stephen Cambone, who may have more influence over intelligence matters at Pentagon than anyone who has previously tried to oversee enterprise; Cambone, who admitted giving high-level impetus to overhaul of interrogation procedures at prisons in Iraq not long before abuses took place, has irritated intelligence agencies and uniformed military services (M)

2004-05-12.title: Israeli Soldiers Ambushed as Dispute Continues Over Bodies
2004-05-12.abstract: An Israeli military vehicle reportedly exploded today in Gaza Strip, killing at least five Israeli soldiers.

2004-05-12.title: A Flurry Of Diplomacy In Asia on Eve Of Arms Talks
2004-05-12.abstract: Pieces in Northeast Asia's long frozen security puzzle are starting to shift as envoys from United States, China, Japan, Russia and North and South Korea converge in Beijing for midlevel talks on North Korea's nuclear arms program; changes include rumored plan for Japanese Prime Min Junichiro Koizumi to visit North Korea and planned meeting of South and North Korean army generals; moves come in face of US pressure for united front on nuclear arms program (M)

2004-05-12.title: Foreigners Try to Melt an Inhospitable Japanese City
2004-05-12.abstract: A few foreign activists battle reluctance of bars and other entertainment establishments in Mombetsu, Japan, to admit foreigners; nation has treaty obligation to end discrimination, but has dragged its feet on carrying out law; bar owners blame bad behavior by Russians; other factors noted; map; photo (M)

2004-05-12.title: World Briefing | Americas: Colombia: Ex-Soldiers Among Plot Suspects
2004-05-12.abstract: Colombian army General Martin Carremo reports at least 26 of nearly 100 people arrested for alleged plot to oust President Hugo Chavez had once served in Colombia's armed forces; photo (S)

2004-05-13.title: Harsh C.I.A. Methods Cited In Top Qaeda Interrogations
2004-05-13.abstract: Central Intelligence Agency's use of coercive interrogation methods against some high-level leaders and operatives of Al Qaeda produces concern inside agency about abuse; at least one agency employee reportedly has been disciplined for threatening detainee with gun during questioning; Khalid Shaikh Mohammed, thought to have helped plan 9/11 terror attacks, was strapped down, forcibly pushed under water and made to believe he might drown; that and other techniques were authorized by set of secret rules for interrogation of high-level Qaeda prisoners that were endorsed by Justice Dept and CIA; rules are among first adopted by Bush administration after 9/11 for handling detainees and may have helped establish new understanding throughout government that officials would have greater freedom to deal harshly with detainees; methods used by CIA are so severe that senior officials of Federal Bureau of Investigation directed its agents to stay out of interviews for fear of being compromised in future criminal cases; defenders of operation say methods do not violate American anti-torture statutes, and are necessary to fight war against nebulous enemy whose intentions could only be gleaned by extracting information from uncooperative detainees; photo (M)

2004-05-13.title: Reporter's Expulsion Brings Mounting Criticism in Brazil
2004-05-13.abstract: Brazilian opposition leaders, press associations and legal and judicial groups protest government's decision to expel New York Times correspondent Larry Rohter for article on Pres Luiz Inicio Lula da Silva's reported drinking habit; da Silva calls article 'malicious assault' on presidency and says he will not consider revoking action; Brazilian newspapers report that Brazilian diplomats are working to change da Silva's mind; New York Times says it believes Rohter's reporting was accurate and that it will oppose move to expel him (M)

2004-05-13.title: LAWMAKERS VIEW IMAGES FROM IRAQ
2004-05-13.abstract: Lawmakers who viewed hundreds of images of mistreatment of Iraqi prisoners say photos were more graphic than they expected, and included pictures of forced sexual acts between male detainees, soldier posing with body of dead prisoner, and consensual sex between American soldiers; lawmakers emerge from viewing shaken and aghast; say they could not tell where pictures were taken, but images of mistreatment appear to be confined to Abu Ghraib prison because people in photo are ones seen in photos released to public; Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld defends interrogation techniques used in Iraq, saying Defense Dept lawyers deemed them to be consistent with Geneva Conventions; Sen Richard Durbin disputes this claim; explicit nature of photographs leaves lawmakers deeply conflicted over whether images should be made public; even some who previously favored public release, like Sen Charles E Schumer, say they changed their minds after military personnel said public release would violate prisoners' right to privacy and protection from humiliation under international law; photos (M)

2004-05-13.title: In a 2nd Attack, Gazans Kill 5 Israeli Soldiers; 7 Arabs Die
2004-05-13.abstract: Palestinian militants destroy Israeli armored vehicle in Gaza Strip, killing five soldiers and confronting troops who storm after their fallen comrades; Israeli helicopter then fires missile at Palestinians army says were trying to attack troops on recovery mission in Rafah refugee camp; seven Palestinians are killed; other Israeli forces in northern Gaza scour Gaza City neighborhood for remains of six soldiers killed in similar incident day earlier; photo (M)

2004-05-13.title: C.I.A. Says Qaeda Militant Decapitated American
2004-05-13.abstract: Abu Musab al-Zarqawi is believed to have beheaded Nicholas Berg in a video clip posted on an Islamist Web site Tuesday.

2004-05-14.title: World Briefing | Europe: Turkey: Disputed Education Bill Passed
2004-05-14.abstract: Turkish Parliament passes education reforms opposed by military and educators, who believe changes promote Islamists moving into secular jobs and government (S)

2004-05-14.title: Congress Seeking to Clarify Iraqis' Role Under Self-Rule
2004-05-14.abstract: Bush administration says leaders of Iraqi interim government will negotiate their own control over security and other matters once they are chosen later this month; administration is seeking to quell questions about limitations on sovereignty of future government there; Congress and United Nations Security Council have demanded that US clear up what powers interim government will have when self-rule is restored on June 30; Under Secretary of State Marc Grossman, who used phrase 'limited sovereignty' last month, now says there will be limits on Iraq's 'authority' but not its sovereignty (M)

2004-05-14.title: China Gives U.S.-Based Activist 5-Year Sentence on Spy Charge
2004-05-14.abstract: Chinese court sentences United States-based political activist Yang Jianli to five years in prison on charges of spying; case has prompted protests by Washington because of Yang's detention without trial for two years; he was detained after he traveled to China to meet with dissidents and labor protesters; he is veteran of 1989 democracy protests in Beijing and had been banned from entering China; he visited using friend's passport (M)

2004-05-14.title: Guard Left Troubled Life for Duty in Iraq
2004-05-14.abstract: Specialist Charles A Graner Jr, who is emerging as central figure in abuse scandal at Abu Ghraib prison, had personal and work problems at home before going to Iraq; brief profile; photo (M)

2004-05-14.title: PREMIER OF INDIA IS FORCED TO QUIT AFTER VOTE UPSET
2004-05-14.abstract: Prime Min Atal Bihari Vajpayee of India resigns after his governing coalition suffers shocking upset in parliamentary elections; his Hindu nationalist party has led India since 1998; peace process with Pakistan that has advanced on trust built between him and Gen Pervez Musharraf could be affected; electoral defeat, which was not forecast by any major poll or pundit, is seen as repudiation of Vajpayee's agenda of privatization and other economic reforms; his message that an ascendant India was banishing poverty was mockery to struggling farmers and legions of unemployed; Indian National Congress led by Sonia Gandhi capitalized on widespread discontent to emerge as single largest party; party appears poised to form country's next government with support of electoral allies and country's Communist parties; Gandhi seems likely to claim prime minister's post, although fact that she was not born in India might stand in way; she is widow of former Prime Min Rajiv Gandhi; photo (M)

2004-05-15.title: Nuclear Monitor Sees Treaties Weakening
2004-05-15.abstract: International Atomic Energy Agency Dir Mohamed ElBaradei tells Council on Foreign Relations that intricate web of treaties and agreements that limit spread of nuclear weapons was weakening and could be endangered unless sweeping reforms to system were made in United Nations Security Council and elsewhere; says he and Pres Bush have discussed working jointly to bolster Nuclear Nonproliferation Treaty and on other reforms that are crucial to stopping spread of nuclear, biological and chemical weapons (M)

2004-05-15.title: Japan to Press North Korea On Kidnapping
2004-05-15.abstract: Prime Min Junichiro Koizumi will travel to North Korea in attempt to win release of family members of Japanese citizens abducted by North; it will be second visit to Pyongyang by Koizumi, whose first trip in Sept 2002 led to repatriation of five Japanese abductees (M)

2004-05-15.title: World Briefing | Americas: Canada: Move To Export Cheap Drugs
2004-05-15.abstract: Canadian Parliament approves legislation allowing drug companies to produce and export generic drugs to poor countries battling health crises, including AIDS (S)

2004-05-15.title: Britain to Start Direct Sale Of an Anti-Cholesterol Drug
2004-05-15.abstract: Britain will become first country to sell cholesterol-reducing drug, called statin, without prescription; low dosage of drug Zocor will be sold over counter starting in July to people at moderate risk of heart disease, number that could reach 5 million to 10 million; drug is made by Johnson and Johnson MSD (M)

2004-05-15.title: Finding a Region's Soul in an Accordion's Wail
2004-05-15.abstract: Profile of Andres Gil, accordion player who teaches 400 students at his musical academy in Valledupar, Colombia, traditional vallenato music of region; Gil waives tuition for those who cannot pay, and he travels countryside, like wandering minstrel, giving lessons to 300 more students in isolated communities; most of his students are children; photo (M)

2004-05-16.title: Israeli Army Kills 3 Palestinians on Tense Day in Gaza
2004-05-16.abstract: The Palestinians were killed as they tried to cross the border into Israel from the Gaza Strip, an Israeli military security official said.

2004-05-16.title: North Korean Performers Adapt to Life on a New Stage
2004-05-16.abstract: North Korean performers who defect to South Korea confront cultural divide; typically perform traditional songs and dances that are popular with older people but no longer comprehensible to younger South Koreans raised on latest pop music; North Korean performers typically work together in large groups according to rigid artistic system of North Korea, while South Korea rewards individualism and originality; photo; map (M)

2004-05-16.title: Israeli Crowd Supports Pullout From Gaza
2004-05-16.abstract: Crowd of 120,000 people in Tel Aviv rallies to support Israeli Prime Min Ariel Sharon's plan for unilateral withdrawal from Gaza in rare display of support for conservative government from marginalized left; Sharon's own Likud Party rejected plan in referendum two weeks ago; Shimon Perez of center-left Labor Party says 80 percent of Israelis support plan and will not allow one percent of population represented by Likud dissenters to derail it; photo (M)

2004-05-16.title: Powell Offers Abuse Apology and Israeli Rebuke
2004-05-16.abstract: There was little evidence that the twin gestures had mollified Arabs mistrustful of the U.S. in both Iraq and the Mideast.

2004-05-16.title: Powell, on Trip to Mideast, Vows Justice on Iraq Abuse
2004-05-16.abstract: Sec of State Colin L Powell, in Shuneh, Jordan, addresses members of Arab world's business and political elite at meeting of World Economic Forum; promises that justice will be done in matter of prisoner abuses at Iraq's Abu Ghraib prison; renews commitments to progress in Middle East peace efforts and to working with United Nations in establishing new independent Iraqi administration; asks Arabs not to lose sight of good work of Americans in rebuilding Iraq; receives polite applause; photo (M)

2004-05-17.title: Health Care Leads Other Issues in Canadian Vote
2004-05-17.abstract: Canada's publicly financed health insurance system emerges as dominant issue of imminent national election campaign; although majority of Canadians still view their universal coverage system with patriotic pride, satisfaction with medical services has declined in last decade as population ages, waiting lines for care grow and doctors and nurses become sparse, especially in rural areas; Prime Min Paul Martin promises to 'provide a fix' that will last for generation; he is being challenged on right by Stephen Harper, who contends that provinces should be given leeway to experiment with private sector delivery of vital health serivces (M)

2004-05-17.title: Iraqi Council Leader Is Killed in Blast Near U.S. Headquarters
2004-05-17.abstract: It was unclear if the victim, who this month held the council's rotating presidency, was the specific target of the suicide bomber.

2004-05-17.title: SOME IRAQIS HELD OUTSIDE CONTROL OF TOP GENERAL
2004-05-17.abstract: About 100 high-ranking Iraqi prisoners held for months in spartan conditions near Baghdad International Airport are being detained under special chain of command, in conditions not subject to approval by top American commander in Iraq; tangled network of authority over prisoners in Iraq involves military police, military intelligence, Central Intelligence Agency, Defense Intelligence Agency, various military commanders and Pentagon itself; Congressional investigators looking into scandal over abuse of Iraqi prisoners say those arrangements make it difficult to determine where final authority lies; Red Cross report states that many of 100 'high-value detainees' are being held in conditions that violate Geneva Conventions; Bush administration says it regards conventions as 'fully applicable' to all prisoners held by US in Iraq; Defense Dept officials say principal responsibility for high-value prisoners and their treatment belongs to Iraq Survey Group headed by Maj Gen Keith Dayton of DIA; survey group was formed to hunt for Iraq's illicit weapons and to gather information about Iraqi war crimes; for weapons hunt, survey group falls under overall authority of CIA; on other matters it reports to Central Command under Gen John P Abizaid; high-value detainees being held near airport include Tariq Aziz and other former senior officials, but not Saddam Hussein (M)

2004-05-17.title: American Airstrike Hits Insurgents Near Mosque in Karbala
2004-05-17.abstract: KARBALA, Iraq, May 17 — In its riskiest attack yet against the forces of a rebel Shiite cleric, the American military called in an airstrike today to kill fighters standing about 160 feet away from one of the holiest shrines in Shiite Islam, military officials said.

2004-05-17.title: U.S. Finds Shell With Nerve Gas in Iraq
2004-05-17.abstract: The explosive containing nerve gas had been rigged as a roadside bomb and caused no casualties, the military said.

2004-05-18.title: Israeli Army Moves on Rafah Refugee Camp in Gaza
2004-05-18.abstract: Israeli armored bulldozers reinforced by helicopter gunships advance into Rafah refugee camp in southern Gaza Strip after Israeli troops seal camp off for what army calls large-scale operation to root out militants and weapons-smuggling tunnels; frightened Palestinians flee camp as troops and tanks draw cordon around it; other residents of camp pick through wreckage of dozens of homes smashed by Israeli forces during fierce fighting last week; raid follows killings last week of 13 Israeli soldiers in Gaza, including seven in Rafah area, and 32 Palestinians; national security adviser Condoleezza Rice offers mild criticism of Israel's demolition plan in Rafah, but European Union foreign ministers condemn action and UN Secretary General Kofi Annan appeals to Israeli government to stop destruction; photo (M)

2004-05-18.title: Nettuno Journal; A Tiny Corner of Italy That Hasn't Forgotten America
2004-05-18.abstract: More than 7,000 Americans who died in Italy in World War II are buried in cemetery in Nettuno, near Rome; each lies buried with marble headstone that is cleaned twice a year, by hand, with pumice stone and soap; grounds are lovingly cared for; gratitude felt for Americans there is palpable, even after all these years; photo; map (M)

2004-05-18.title: Old Iraq Army Could Provide A Leader, Jordan's King Says
2004-05-18.abstract: King Abdullah II of Jordan, key player in American diplomacy in Middle East, says that after US hands over formal sovereignty to Iraqis on June 30, country should be run by strongman--possibly drawn from ranks of Saddam Hussein's army, interview; seems to be registering disapproval of former Iraqi exiles at core of current leadership; says although Iraq's military elite was made up of Hussein loyalists, some older officers might not be tainted in same way as those on America's wanted list; king's remarks seem to run counter to American insistence on ridding Iraqi public of of former elite, but they reflect his growing concern that Iraq is heading for deeper strife, possibly civil war, before elections planned for next Jan; speaking of Israeli-Palestinian conflict, king suggests that Yasir Arafat should yield authority, demand rarely voiced so openly in Arab world; Jordan is in complicated position because majority of its people are of Palestinian descent; Abdullah must balance his close alliance with US and emotions of population increasingly hostile to American policy (M)

2004-05-18.title: At Least 100 Are Killed In Prison Fire In Honduras
2004-05-18.abstract: More than 100 gang members in severely overcrowded prison in San Pedro Sula, Honduras, die in early-morning fire apparently caused by electrical short circuit; this is second major prison disaster in country in 13 months; 68 people died in Apr 2003 in ramshackle jail at El Porvenir, 51 of them executed by state police, soldiers, guards and prisoners working as trusties; no evidence available suggests foul play in fire, but in both disasters almost all of dead are members of violent youth gang that has created contining law enforcement crisis in country; photo (M)

2004-05-18.title: Views Mixed On U.S. Shift On Drugs For AIDS
2004-05-18.abstract: AIDS activists and doctors who treat patients in poor countries greet Bush administration's shift in its policy on procuring AIDS drugs with mixed reviews; many are delighted, but some express frustration that White House has set up new approval process overseen by Food and Drug Administration when one overseen by World Health Orgn already exists; they consider this another roadblock (M)

2004-05-19.title: Morning-After Pill in Canada: Prescription May Not Be Needed
2004-05-19.abstract: Canadian government proposes to allow women to obtain 'morning-after' birth control pill without prescription at any pharmacy (S)

2004-05-19.title: GANDHI DECLINES TOP POST IN INDIA
2004-05-19.abstract: Sonia Gandhi, Italian-born widow and daughter-in-law of slain prime ministers, stuns India by announcing she will 'humbly decline' to be prime minister, saying that has never been her aim in leading Congress Party to election victory over Hindu nationalist party; announcement sets off protests as emotional as those of Hindu nationalists who object to her foreign birth; photo; stock market rallies after plummeting over fears that Congress Party will reverse economic reforms; Gandhi reportedly favors Manmohan Singh, respected former finance minister, to lead country; adviser says she never wanted to be prime minister and entered politics in 1998 to defeat Hindu nationalists she viewed as threat to secular state (M)

2004-05-19.title: Fellow Civilian Prison Guards Defend an Accused Sergeant
2004-05-19.abstract: Fellow civilian guards at Buckingham Correctional Center in rural Virginia defend Staff Sgt Ivan L Frederick II, accused of being central figure in abuse of prisoners at Abu Ghraib prison in Iraq; map (S)

2004-05-19.title: U.S. Barred Legal Review Of Detentions, Lawyer Says
2004-05-19.abstract: Military lawyers, who had active role in supervising interrogations in first Gulf War, have been excluded in Iraq, which human rights lawyer Scott Horton says may have played role in abuse of detainees at Abu Ghraib prison; photo; he and other attorneys say exclusion of Judge Advocate General's Corps suggests lack of concern with legal standards by Bush administration (M)

2004-05-19.title: Israeli Shells Hit Crowd of Palestinians, Killing at Least 9
2004-05-19.abstract: Palestinians called it an unprovoked attack; Israeli military officials said there were gunmen in the crowd.

2004-05-20.title: White House Considers Plan to Let Iraqi Forces Opt Out of Military Operations Ordered by the U.S.
2004-05-20.abstract: Bush administration is considering plan to allow Iraqi security forces to decline to take part in military operations ordered by American commanders, in response to concerns at United Nations about limitations on Iraq's future sovereignty; officials hope so-called 'opt-out' arrangement will help win support at Security Council for resolution conferring legitimacy on caretaker government to be installed in Iraq after June 30; photo (M)

2004-05-20.title: French Pedophilia Case Falls Apart When Main Suspect Recants
2004-05-20.abstract: Pedophilia case that has captivated France, and destroyed lives of dozen people, collapses when main suspect, Myriam Delay, suddenly admits that she made up much of story; on 10th day of her trial, she tells stunned courtroom that her tale of gang rapes and child prostitution ring based in her home in Outreau was elaborate lie; points out several of accused, saying they are innocent; trial has shattered lives of 18 people accused in case, with one committing suicide and others losing custody of their children; Delay could face up to 2 years in jail for her false accusations, in addition to possible sentence of 20 years for sexual abuse of minors (M)

2004-05-20.title: Two U.S. Generals Outline a Lag in Notification on Reports of Abuse in Iraqi Prison
2004-05-20.abstract: Two top commanders of American forces in Iraq, testifying before Senate Armed Services Committee, say Red Cross reports of prisoner abuse last year at Abu Ghraib prison did not reach them for months after complaints were submitted to their subordinates; Lt Gen Ricardo S Sanchez says he has ordered that Red Cross reports now be shown to senior commanders as soon as they are received; Gen John P Abizaid says he did not learn of broader report Red Cross submittedin Feb until early May; referring to way Red Cross reports are handled by military, he says 'system is broken'; Abizaid says preliminary inquiry by Army inspector general found problems with training, organization and doctine regarding military detention centers in Iraq and Afghanistan, but no 'pattern of abuse' of prisoners in Central Command's area of responsibility; photo (M)

2004-05-20.title: Top Arafat Aide Convicted by Israelis on Murder Charges
2004-05-20.abstract: The prosecution demanded that Marwan Barghouti be sentenced to life imprisonment for each of the five killings.

2004-05-20.title: Names of the Dead
2004-05-20.abstract: Defense Dept confirms deaths of three service members in Iraq (S)

2004-05-21.title: World Briefing | Europe: Greece: Bomb Alert At American School
2004-05-21.abstract: Anonymous caller warning of bomb provokes Athens police to seal off American school in suburb of Agia Paraskevi; school is planned training facility for US athletes during 2004 Olympics; no bomb is found (S)

2004-05-21.title: World Briefing | Europe: Turkey: Blast At McDonald's, But No Injuries
2004-05-21.abstract: Bomb explodes in Istanbul (Turkey) McDonald's parking lot causing damage, but no injuries (S)

2004-05-21.title: World Briefing | Africa: Malawi: Vote For A New Leader
2004-05-21.abstract: Malawi parliamentary elections, which are nation's third democratic elections since ending three decades of dictatorship in 1994, draw large number of voters; President Bakili Muluzi is retiring after 10 years as leader and new Parliament will choose his successor (S)

2004-05-21.title: NATO Flirts With Failure in Afghanistan
2004-05-21.abstract: NATO has cast its decision to send forces to help stabilize Afghanistan as a vital step in its struggle for relevance.

2004-05-21.title: Chinese People's Republic Is Unfair to Its Short People
2004-05-21.abstract: Chinese government denies government jobs to those who do not meet often unwritten code of fitness--height, looks and robust good health--that many say has nothing to do with their ability to perform task in question; standards are sign of how far China has drifted from Communist Party's stated socialist values; officials are no longer selected on basis of ideological fervor or working-class background, but rather in ways that seem more reminiscent of China's imperial history; rigorous requirements for height and physique have long been used to screen people who want to work in China's most internationally visible jobs; new effort to extend those standards to all government jobs is traced to China's effort to put best face forward since joining World Trade Orgn and interacting more with outside world; photo; map (M)

2004-05-22.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Chechnya Sets Election Date
2004-05-22.abstract: Chechen officials announce Aug 29 election to determine successor to Akhmad Kadyrov, who was recently killed in Grozny bomb attack; pro-Moscow Prime Minister Sergei B Abramov is acting president (S)

2004-05-22.title: A New Strategy Document Calls Attention to the Transition Between War and Peace
2004-05-22.abstract: Joint Chiefs of Staff Chmn Richard B Myers approves new national military strategy that makes clear to nation's war planners that they must pay greater attention to preparing for complicated and dangerous transition between end of major combat operations and return of civil authority in Iraq (M)

2004-05-22.title: Only a Few Spoke Up on Abuse As Many Soldiers Stayed Silent
2004-05-22.abstract: Army Specialist Joseph M Darby says he told investigators about mistreatment of Iraqi prisoners at Abu Ghraib after he decided that it was 'morally wrong'; Darby stands out from other soldiers who learned of abuse; some soldiers say they tried to report misconduct or stop it, but nothing came of their efforts; Darby's report would initiate investigation into mistreatment of prisoners at Abu Ghraib and other military facilities in Iraq and raise questions about whether abuse was authorized by military officials; seven soldiers charged in investigation are all from Darby's unit, 372nd Military Police Co; Staff Sgt Ivan L Frederick II is described as ringleader of misconduct; Darby photo (M)

2004-05-22.title: 5 Afghans Die and 4 U.S. Soldiers Are Wounded in Clashes
2004-05-22.abstract: Five Afghans are killed and four US soldiers are wounded in clashes in Afghanistan; Pakistani officials protest what they say was second incursion by US forces into Pakistan in month (M)

2004-05-22.title: U.S. Preparing for Influx of Compensation Claims by Abused Iraqis
2004-05-22.abstract: Defense Dept officials are preparing for influx of compensation claims stemming from charges of abuse at Abu Ghraib prison in Iraq (M)

2004-05-23.title: Qaddafi Stalks Out of an Arab Assembly
2004-05-23.abstract: Libyan leader Col Muammar el-Qaddafi walks out of summit meeting of Arab leaders in Tunis; Qaddafi has repeatedly called for dismantling of Arab League and had to be coaxed to attend meeting; announces he is withdrawing from meeting because entire agenda is flawed; says Arab governments and Arab people have different agendas; Qaddafi's outbursts, while erratic, often reflect popular sentiment; latest move appears to reflect general Arab frustration over occupation of Iraq; photo (M)

2004-05-23.title: IRAQ UPDATE
2004-05-23.abstract:  In Baghdad, a car bomb outside the home of an Iraqi deputy interior minister, Abdul-Jabbar Youssef al-Sheikhli, wounded him and killed at least five people  four security guards and a passer-by.

2004-05-23.title: Roof Collapses at Paris Airport, Killing 5
2004-05-23.abstract: A 120-foot section of a new terminal at Paris' Charles de Gaulle airport collapsed early Sunday, killing at least five people.

2004-05-23.title: THE ORGAN TRADE: A Global Black Market; Tracking the Sale of a Kidney On a Path of Poverty and Hope
2004-05-23.abstract: Sums being offered on black market for sale of kidneys for transplant seem like fortune to people mired in poverty; in parts of Brazil where monthly wage is barely $80, giving up kidney for $6,000 represents huge opportunity--more than decade's wages; donors and recipients are linked by trafficking ring that authorities say exploits two very different needs: for money and for life itself; journey of kidney sold by Alberty Jose da Silva of Recife, Brazil, to American woman in Brooklyn, NY, by way of intermediary in Israel and surgery in South African hospital traced; final cost to recipient is $60,000; da Silva and other Brazilian kidney donors have seen their payments jeopardized when they were arrested and briefly jailed on suspicion of violating laws against trading in human organs; photos; map (L)

2004-05-23.title: Moore's 'Fahrenheit 9/11' Wins Top Honors at Cannes
2004-05-23.abstract: Cannes Film Festival awards Palm d'Or to Michael Moore's movie Fahrenheit 9/11; movie, highly critical of Bush administration's foreign policies, particularly war in Iraq, is one of only three non-fiction films allowed in competition in nearly 50 years; Moore, who says he did not set out to make political film, is still looking for American distributor for movie (M)

2004-05-24.title: Names of the Dead
2004-05-24.abstract: Defense Dept confirms death of service member in Iraq (S)

2004-05-24.title: Impact of Sony-BMG merger is at issue : EU is taking a long look at puzzling cost of music
2004-05-24.abstract: PARIS:—  At the heart of the European Commission's review of the proposed merger of Sony Music and BMG, the recording company owned by Bertelsmann, is the search for a satisfactory response to one question: Where do music prices come from?

2004-05-24.title: General Says Sanchez Rejected Her Offer to Give Address to Iraqis About Abuses
2004-05-24.abstract: Brig Gen Janis Karpinski, former head of military police at Abu Ghraib, says Lt Gen Ricardo S Sanchez, top American general in Iraq, rejected her recommendation in Jan that military make public Arabic-language radio or television address to Iraqi people to confront accusations of abuse at prison; says Sanchez visited military intelligence unit at Abu Ghraib at least three times in Oct, when first of worst of abuses were taking place; Sanchez has said he did not learn of abuses until Jan 14, but Karpinski says that in late Nov, Sanchez's top deputy Maj Gen Walter Wojdakowski was present at meeting at which there was extensive discussion of Red Cross report that cited specific cases of abuse; two Defense Dept offiicals acknowledge that command in Baghdad was reluctant to say too much at outset because of continuing criminal investigation; photos (M)

2004-05-24.title: Beijing Journal; China Warms Up and Practices for a Future in Baseball
2004-05-24.abstract: Chinese Baseball League All-Stars, China's first professional baseball league, is in its second season; few fans turn out and players lack skills, but promoters are optimistic; country is gearing up to host 2008 Olympics in Beijing, where it will compete in sport for first time; photo (M)

2004-05-24.title: Inquiry on Secrets
2004-05-24.abstract: Investigators are checking whether U.S. officials gave secrets to Ahmad Chalabi, the Iraqi suspected of passing them to Iran. Page A11.

2004-05-25.title: Excerpts From Bush Speech on American Strategy in Iraq
2004-05-25.abstract: Excerpts from Pres Bush's speech on American strategy in Iraq (M)

2004-05-25.title: C.I.A. Bid to Keep Some Detainees Off Abu Ghraib Roll Worries Officials
2004-05-25.abstract: Central Intelligence Agency's practice of keeping some detainees in Abu Ghraib prison off official rosters so concerned top Army officer and civilian official there that they reached written agreement early this year to stop; undated memorandum is described as agreement between Army intelligence unit assigned to prison and 'external agencies,' euphemism for CIA, to halt practices that bypass both military rules and international standards; Maj Gen Antonio M Taguba, Army officer who first investigated prison abuses, complained that military guards were being enlisted to hide prisoners from Red Cross; memorandum provides clearest indication to date that military officials were troubled by practice even before Taguba's report; Lt Gen James R Helmly, leader of Army Reserve, suspends Brig Gen Janis L Karpinski, commander of military police brigade at Abu Ghraib, pending review of misconduct there while she was commander of prison; photo (M)

2004-05-25.title: Russian Scientist Dies in Ebola Accident at Former Weapons Lab
2004-05-25.abstract: Russian scientist at former Soviet biological weapons laboratory in Siberia dies after accidentally sticking herself with needle laced with deadly ebola virus, for which there is no vaccine or treatment; scientists and officials say accident raises concerns about safety and secrecy at State Research Center of Virology and Biotechnology, leading recipient of aid in American program to help former Soviet scientists and labs convert to peaceful research; American experts say accident did not occur in lab now receiving US government or private money for research (M)

2004-05-25.title: Sharon Reportedly Will Avoid Bribery Charges
2004-05-25.abstract: Israeli television stations are reporting that Atty Gen Menachem Mazuz has decided against indicting Prime Min Ariel Sharon on charges of accepting bribes from real estate developer; threat of indictment has been political handicap for Sharon as he tries to cajole and press ministers in his coalition government to support his plan to 'disengage' from Palestinians without peace agreement; Mazuz, whose spokesman refuses to confirm TV reports, is expected to make formal announcement of his decision on June 15 (M)

2004-05-25.title: IRAQ UPDATE
2004-05-25.abstract: Update of news from Iraq; map (S)

2004-05-26.title: India's Coalition Survives Battle Over Cabinet
2004-05-26.abstract: Congress Party yields to demands of one of its coalition partners for more cabinet positions, staving off short-term threat to cohesion of Indian Prime Min Manmohan Singh's new government but raising questions about its long-term stability; gives most of key posts to longtime party stalwarts known for loyalty to family of party leader Sonia Gandhi; must cater to demands of coalition partners who share little more than dislike for recently defeated Hindu nationalist party and love for power (M)

2004-05-26.title: Shrine in Najaf Damaged Again as Clashes Continue
2004-05-26.abstract: Shrine of Imam Ali, one of Shiite Muslims' holiest sites, suffers minor damage for second time in May as clashes continue between American forces and insurgents loyal to rebel cleric Moktada al-Sadr in Najaf and neighboring Kufa; American soldiers have taken great pains to avoid damaging shrine for fear of alienating Shiites; Gen Mark Kimmitt says it is unclear whether damage was caused by fighting between rival Shiite factions or intentionally by Sadr's men to blame Americans; Sadr's force blame American fire; Americans say Sadr's men may have tried to reoccupy mosque in downtown Karbala, but apparently ran off as American armored vehicles approached; bomb blast near Baghdad hotel wounds two people; second blast wounds American soldier; photos; diagram (M)

2004-05-26.title: Pakistani Officer Dies in Blast Near English-Language School
2004-05-26.abstract: One policeman was killed and at least 26 people were injured today when two car bombs went off within an interval of 25 minutes in the southern port city of Karachi.

2004-05-26.title: IRAQ UPDATE
2004-05-26.abstract: Update of events in Iraq war; map (M)

2004-05-26.title: Candidates' Iraq Policies Share Many Similarities
2004-05-26.abstract: Pres Bush and Sen John Kerry differ little on broad outlines of their approaches to Iraq, although Kerry holds Bush has lost international credibility and that only new president can rally other nations to provide needed help; Bush's gradual shift toward policies advocated by Kerry makes it hard for Kerry to exploit what Democrats and Republicans view as Bush's biggest political crisis by stressing differences between them; chart compares their positions (M)

2004-05-27.title: Top Candidate to Lead Iraq's Interim Government Says He Doesn't Want the Job
2004-05-27.abstract: Dr Hussain al-Shahristani, Iraqi nuclear scientist who spent several years in Abu Ghraib prison after breaking with Saddam Hussein over plans to develop atomic bomb, withdraws his name from consideration as interim leader of Iraq, saying he prefers to serve his country in other ways; he escaped to West in 1991; separately, American military announces capture in Najaf of Riyadh al-Nouri, Moktada al-Sadr's brother-in-law and crucial figure in Sadr's organization in Najaf; Nouri is handed over to Iraqi authorities for prosecution in connection with Apr 2003 murder of rival of Sadr; fierce fighting breaks out in Najaf between Sadr's militias and American troops; photo (M)

2004-05-27.title: Names of the Dead
2004-05-27.abstract: Defense Dept confirms deaths of four service members in Iraq (S)

2004-05-27.title: Two Shrines Intact, but U.S Reputation Is Marred After Clashes With Rebels
2004-05-27.abstract: Shrine of Imam Hussein survives month of urban fighting in Karbala, but American reputation is marred; Iraqis seem to be angry not at insurgent army that took shelter in city, but at American army; they point to devastation wrought by three weeks of fighting, evident in wrecked hotels and bullet-riddled cars and ruined elementary school; before American army arrrived in Karbala, many of locals, especially businessmen, had grown tired of Mahdi Army, as had some of country's most powerful political and clerical leaders; they sent series of signals that many interpreted as green light for Americans to move into Karbala and Najaf with explicit condition that they stay out of two holy shrines and avoid damaging them; in fact, religious shrines received only minor damage, testament to what appears to be remarkable marksmanship by Americans; photos; maps (M)

2004-05-27.title: World Briefing | Asia: Indonesia: Terror Expert Facing Expulsion
2004-05-27.abstract: Indonesian International Crisis Group leader Sidney Jones' work permit has expired and Indonesian government will not renew it; group has issued reports criticizing government (S)

2004-05-27.title: Panama Journal; Panama Canal Thinks Big, Bringing Fear to the Jungle
2004-05-27.abstract: Planned renovation of Panama Canal so it could accommodate longer vessels might devastate poverty-stricken villages nearby; thousands of residents, most of them Indian, would be forced off their land; Indian activists and church groups have campaigned for months in villages to inform peasant farmers about proposed expansion of canal and educate them about their rights; photos; map (M)

2004-05-28.title: Sharon, in Reversal, Proposes Full Pullout From Gaza Strip
2004-05-28.abstract: Prime Minister Ariel Sharon of Israel has decided to present ministers with his original plan for a complete withdrawal.

2004-05-28.title: It's Out of College and Onto Jobless Rolls in China
2004-05-28.abstract: Even college-educated applicants cannot find jobs in China, where leaders are struggling to control country's overheating economy; country's economy must roar just to keep unemployment from rising; putting brakes on growth, even gently, will increase unemployment rate; political pressure on Communist Party to continue creating jobs is enormous; more than 110 million migrant workers have left countryside for cities in search of jobs in factories or on construction sites; transition from planned economy to market economy cost tens of millions of workers their jobs with closings of inefficient state-owned enterprises; photos (M)

2004-05-28.title: World Briefing | Americas: Canada: Inuit Vote For Autonomy
2004-05-28.abstract: Nunatsiavut, Inuit region in northern Labrador (Canada), citizens vote in favor of land claim agreement giving them governing rights for 28,000 square miles territory; was last outstanding Inuit land claim in Canada (S)

2004-05-28.title: World Briefing | Africa: Congo: Army Factions Clash
2004-05-28.abstract: Former enemies that are now part of Congo's national army exchange gunfire in Bukavu when ex-leader of Rally for Congolese Democracy accuses soldiers loyal to Pres Joseph Kabila of trying to arrest him; United Nations peacekeepers report three people have died (S)

2004-05-29.title: Troops Clash With Militia, Threatening Day-Old Truce
2004-05-29.abstract: Battles break out in Kufa, Iraq, killing at least five militiamen, day after declaration of truce between American soldiers and forces loyal to rebel Shiite cleric Moktada al-Sadr; each side accuses other of shooting first; both say they will honor truce; large bands of militiamen armed with AK-47's and heavy weapons roam streets of Kufa and neighboring Najaf; al-Sadr does not appaear at his Kufa mosque to deliver weekly sermon; his aide speaks instead, denouncing Americans; photo (M)

2004-05-29.title: Suspect in Reporter's Death Is Wanted in Attacks on Musharraf
2004-05-29.abstract: Pakistani intelligence officials says Amjad Hussain Farooqi, Pakistani militant who is believed to have planned and carried out twin assassination attempts on Pres Pervez Musharraf in Dec, is top fugitive wanted in 2002 murder of American journalist Daniel Pearl; says data from American intelligence agencies, including information from Khalid Shaikh Mohammed, captured Al Qaeda leader, helped Pakistani investigators track down ring involved in assassination; says some 10 low-level military officers have been arrested in plot (M)

2004-05-29.title: Names of the Dead
2004-05-29.abstract: List of Americans newly confirmed dead in Iraq

2004-05-29.title: To Frenzied Scenes, Abu Ghraib Frees 624 Prisoners
2004-05-29.abstract: The prisoner release was the fourth from Abu Ghraib this month and the largest since the publication of photographs showing U.S. soldiers abusing prisoners.

2004-05-29.title: World Briefing | Asia: India: Party Admits Slogans Hurt
2004-05-29.abstract: Former Deputy Prime Minister L K Advani, speaking for first time since Bharatiya Janata Party's election defeat, admits slogans were inappropriate given India's difficult economic times (S)

2004-05-30.title: Ethiopia's Bold Plan to Improve Life Makes It Worse
2004-05-30.abstract: Ethiopia's effort to break cycle of starvation by relocating 2.2 million struggling farmers from highlands to more fertile Oromia lowlands has been disaster; program is well-intentioned, but poorly planned; government hoped to fix problems caused by farmers' unorganized migrations in search of more fertile land; Oromia settlers complain of malaria, hunger and water shortages; many are abandoning new homes to return to old homes, worse off than before; United Nations and donor governments were skeptical from start but are now being asked to help support settlers; photo, map (M)

2004-05-30.title: IRAQ UPDATE
2004-05-30.abstract: Update of events in Iraq; unpublished Army report from Nov 5 mentions inappropriate detentions and violations of Geneva Conventions; American forces fight Mahdi Army in Kufa; Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld says Iraqi guerrillas seek to block self-rule; United Nations envoy Lakhdar Brahimi may face deadlock on choice of Iraq's interim president (M)

2004-05-30.title: U.S. Troops and Rebel Militia Clash Again
2004-05-30.abstract: American troops clash with Mahdi Army of Moktada al-Sadr in Kufa for second day since cease-fire was declared; al-Sadr's spokesman blames US for provoking trouble and sending in Iraqi police from outside, but military says Mahdi Army fired rocket-propelled grenades at patrol, which returned fire and killed attackers; photo (M)

2004-05-30.title: Saudi Commandos Free Hostages, Ending Standoff With Militants
2004-05-30.abstract: Most of the dozens of foreigners who had been trapped by four armed militants were freed, but details of the rescue and the mopping up operation remained murky.

2004-05-30.title: Clashes in Najaf and Kufa Put the Cease-Fire in Peril
2004-05-30.abstract: A cease-fire between U.S. forces and insurgents loyal to a rebel cleric appeared to be unraveling as fighting erupted Sunday and early Monday in Najaf and Kufa.

2004-05-31.title: Military Completed Death Certificates For 20 Prisoners Only After Months Passed
2004-05-31.abstract: Twenty death certificates for Afghan and Iraqi prisoners who died in US custody were completed by US military medical examiners in ten-day rush in May, even though some of deaths occurred months earlier; many of known investigations into abuses moved glacially until publication of notorious photos of abuse at Abu Ghraib prison; some of cases detailed; photo; chart (M)

2004-05-31.title: Sharon Still One Vote Short in Cabinet Debate on Gaza Plan
2004-05-31.abstract: Prime Min Ariel Sharon's plan to withdraw Jewish settlers from Gaza has support of most Israelis and Pres Bush, but seven-hour cabinet meeting ends with Sharon still apparently one vote short of winning over his own ministers; infighting reopens rivalry with Benjamin Netanyahu, finance minister and former prime minister; photo (M)

2004-05-31.title: Calgary Journal; In a Nation of Hockey Fans, A City Reaches Fever Pitch
2004-05-31.abstract: Boisterous oil town of Calgary is in fever pitch over Calgary Flames, team of also-rans that has emerged from years of lackluster skating and near financial ruin to become first Canadian club to make Stanley Club finals in decade; photos; map (M)

2004-05-31.title: SAUDI MILITARY STORMS COMPLEX TO FREE HOSTAGES
2004-05-31.abstract: Saudi commandos in helicopters storm residential complex in Khobar, freeing most of dozens of foreign hostages trapped by four armed militants for 24 hours; photo; map; government reports 22 people killed by militants and 25 wounded, with militants' leader wounded and captured; three other gunmen use hostages as cover to escape in stolen car; attack on luxurious Oasis Residential Resorts is second brazen assault this month against Saudi oil industry and comes as country tries to increase production to ease record global oil prices; one American known dead; most of others are Indian or Filipino; seven American government workers reportedly eluded capture; assault details are murky; Saudi Amb Prince Bandar bin Sultan says oil production will not be disrupted by terrorists; Al Qaeda group claims responsibility (M)

2004-05-31.title: Gunmen Kill a Leading Pro-Taliban Sunni Cleric in Pakistan
2004-05-31.abstract: Gunmen kill leading pro-Taliban Sunni cleric, Mufti Nizammudin Shamzai, in Karachi, prompting enraged protesters to take to streets; Shamzai, soft-spoken religious scholar in his late 70's, had worked to bring together Pakistan's warring Islamic sects and was ardent supporter of Afghanistan's Taliban; many Taliban commanders attended his schools; photo (M)
